questions.question: What does BLEU measure?
options: N-gram precision (typically 1-4) between hypothesis and reference, with a brevity penalty
questions.explanation: BLEU is the geometric mean of 1-4-gram precision against references, plus a brevity penalty.

questions.question: Why use sacrebleu instead of rolling your own BLEU?
options: It normalizes tokenization so scores are comparable across papers and runs
questions.explanation: sacrebleu freezes tokenization, removing a common source of incomparable BLEU numbers.

questions.question: Which NLLB-specific setting controls the target language during decoding?
options: forced_bos_token_id set to the target language code's token id
questions.explanation: NLLB forces the first decoded token to a target-language code via forced_bos_token_id.

questions.question: Which metric family is the 2026 default for production MT quality where labeled data exists?
options: Learned metrics such as COMET (and BERTScore/BLEURT) trained on human judgment
questions.explanation: Learned metrics like COMET correlate more strongly with human judgment than BLEU/chrF alone.

questions.question: When does chrF tend to be more informative than BLEU?
options: For morphologically rich languages where character-level matches catch inflectional variants BLEU misses
questions.explanation: Character F-score captures partial morphological matches that word-level BLEU undercounts.

questions.question: What is off-target generation in multilingual MT?
options: The model decodes into the wrong target language (e.g. NLLB outputting Spanish when French was requested)
questions.explanation: Off-target generation is common on rare language pairs; a post-translation language-ID check catches it.

questions.question: Why does fine-tuning on a few thousand high-quality domain pairs often beat much larger noisy web data?
options: Quality and domain match dominate volume; noisy parallel data introduces drift and hallucination
Web data is illegal to use
questions.explanation: Clean domain-aligned pairs are the largest production lever; noisy data degrades adaptation.

questions.question: When is an LLM (e.g. GPT-4) likely to outperform a specialized MT model in 2026?
options: Idiomatic content, long context, stylistic adaptation via prompting, or content requiring tone control
Small-language pairs with millions of parallel sentences
questions.explanation: LLMs win on idiomatic, long-context, or style-controlled translation; specialized MT wins on throughput and latency.
